items.q: What does a refractor telescope use to gather light?
items.why: A refractor bends light with lenses, whereas a reflector gathers light with mirrors.

items.q: Why is a larger aperture useful?
items.correct: It collects more light, so you can see fainter objects.
items.why: More light-gathering area reveals dimmer stars; magnification comes from the eyepiece, not the aperture.

items.q: What is the main difference between a refractor and a reflector?
items.correct: A refractor uses lenses; a reflector uses mirrors.
items.misconception: They are exactly the same telescope.
items.random_wrong: A reflector uses no optics at all.
items.why: The two main optical telescope designs differ in whether lenses or mirrors collect the light.

items.q: A bigger aperture lets you see:
items.why: Correct answer: Fainter objects. “Brighter objects only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many main types of optical telescope are there (lens vs mirror)?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
